claims.claim: As the SOC of NCM622 batteries increases, the internal pressure and mass loss notably rise during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The results indicate that as the state of charge increases, the internal pressure and mass loss notable rises.

claims.claim: A higher state-of-charge results in a shorter time for the battery to reach maximum temperature during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: A higher state-of-charge brings shorter time for the battery to reach maximum temperature.

claims.claim: The maximum internal pressure occurs before the peak temperature during thermal runaway, with the time interval between maximum pressure and peak temperature ranging from 20.4 s to 288.5 s depending on SOC.
claims.quote: The maximum pressure commences 20.4 (cid:0) 288.5 s prior to the moment when peak temperature occurs.

claims.claim: A positive correlation exists between internal pressure and mass loss rate during the eruption process of lithium-ion batteries undergoing thermal runaway.
claims.quote: A positive correlation has been identified between internal pressure and the mass loss rate during the eruption process.

claims.claim: Both the venting time and the ejection duration decrease as SOC increases, with venting time decreasing linearly and ejection duration decreasing exponentially.
claims.quote: Fig. 4 illustrated the analysis of venting time and ejection duration across batteries with different SOCs, revealing a linear decrease in venting time and an exponential decline in ejection duration as SOC increases.

claims.claim: Under single-sided heating conditions, a lower heating power density leads to a higher venting temperature, shorter venting time, lower TR onset temperature, higher peak temperature, and reduced TR triggering time.
claims.quote: under single-sided heating conditions, a lower heating power density leads to a higher venting temperature, shorter venting time, lower TR onset temperature, higher peak temperature, and reduced TR triggering time.

claims.claim: Expanding the heating area is more effective in initiating internal thermal reactions and accelerating the TR process compared with simply increasing the heating power density.
claims.quote: Compared with simply increasing the heating power density, expanding the heating area is more effective in initiating internal thermal reactions and accelerating the TR process.

claims.claim: Higher heating power density results in a larger gas yield during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: higher heating power density results in a larger gas yield.

claims.claim: The average heat input of 1157.25 kJ is identified as the thermal threshold for venting (pressure relief valve opening) during thermal runaway of 280 Ah LFP batteries.
claims.quote: this study identifies an average heat input of 1157.25 kJ as the thermal threshold for venting in TR simulations of this type of cell.

claims.claim: The influence of heating power density on thermal runaway behavior primarily occurs after the activation of the pressure relief valve, with minor impact before valve opening.
claims.quote: The influence of heating power density on TR behavior primarily occurs after the activation of the pressure relief valve.

claims.claim: As thermal runaway progresses, the flammable range of the gas mixture gradually narrows, reducing explosion risk.
claims.quote: as TR progresses, the flammable range of the gas mixture gradually narrows, reducing explosion risk.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway, the hydrogen-producing reaction between PVDF and lithium terminates first, followed by olefinic reactions, with CO2 being the dominant gas species in the later stages.
claims.quote: the hydrogen-producing reaction between PVDF and lithium terminates first, followed by the olefinic reactions, with CO2 being the dominant gas species in the later stages.

claims.claim: The peak temperature in Test 2 (15 cm × 15 cm single-side heating, 2.22 W/cm²) reached 346°C, which is significantly lower than the 394°C observed in Test 1 (10 cm × 10 cm single-side heating, 5 W/cm²).
claims.quote: the peak temperature in Test 2 reached 346°C, which is significantly lower than the 394°C observed in Test 1.

claims.claim: The gas volumes generated under the four test conditions follow the order Test 1 (152.28 L) > Test 2 (148.13 L) > Test 3 (138.46 L) > Test 4 (129.04 L), with higher heating power density leading to greater gas generation.
claims.quote: the gas volumes for Tests 1 to 4 are 152.28 L, 148.13 L, 138.46 L, and 129.04 L, respectively. A higher unilateral heating power density leads to increased gas production.

claims.claim: Under single-sided heating conditions, a lower heating power density results in the generation of relatively safer gaseous products during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: under single-sided heating conditions, a lower heating power density results in the generation of relatively safer gaseous products during TR.

claims.claim: Insufficient HFC-227ea increases maximum explosion pressure (Pmax) by 12.43%, 9.77%, and 1.43% under equivalence ratios of 0.7, 1.0, and 1.3, respectively.
claims.quote: a small amount of HFC-227ea increases Pmax by 12.43 %, 9.77 %, and 1.43 % under three representative equivalence ratios, respectively.

claims.claim: The explosion enhancement effect of insufficient HFC-227ea is more pronounced under BVG-deficient (fuel-lean) conditions, with the range of HFC-227ea concentration that triggers enhancement expanding significantly at equivalence ratio of 0.7.
claims.quote: the range of XHFC-227ea that triggers this phenomenon expands significantly at ϕ = 0.7, suggesting that the enhancement is more pronounced under BVG-deficient conditions.

claims.claim: When the volumetric ratios of CO2 to HFC-227ea are 5:5 and 7:3, the explosion enhancement effect is reduced by 37% and 49%, respectively.
claims.quote: When the volumetric ratios of CO2 to HFC-227ea are 5:5 and 7:3, the explosion enhancement effect is reduced by 37 % and 49 %, respectively.

claims.claim: HFC-227ea significantly increases the total quantity of burned gas under all equivalence ratios, with the most pronounced enhancement observed at equivalence ratio of 0.7, and this increase in total burned-gas quantity is identified as the dominant factor in explosion pressure enhancement.
claims.quote: HFC-227ea significantly increases the total quantity of burned gas under all equivalence ratios, with the most pronounced enhancement observed at ϕ = 0.7. In contrast, the increments induced by the M1 and M2 schemes are significantly smaller, indicating that CO2 addition can mitigate the increase in total burned-gas quantity.

claims.claim: Under fuel-rich conditions at equivalence ratio of 1.3, even when HF concentration from HFC-227ea suppression is diluted from the experimental chamber (0.02 m3) to a 10 m3 space, the peak HF value still exceeds the IDLH limit of 30 ppm, reaching 35 ppm.
claims.quote: Even when the observed HF concentration is diluted from the experimental chamber (0.02 m3) to a 10 m3 space, its peak value still exceeds the IDLH limit reaching 35 ppm (ϕ = 1.3).
